%% solve(grid) -> integer()
solve(Grid) ->
    M = length(Grid),
    N = length(lists:at(Grid, 0)),
    
    % DP table: dp[i][j] stores a set of all possible XOR sums to reach (i, j).
    % Since M*N <= 1000, the maximum path length is 1000. 
    % The maximum value in grid is 1023 (10 bits).
    % The number of distinct XOR sums can be large, but since the maximum value is 1023, 
    % the maximum possible XOR sum is also less than 1024.
    % We can use a map or an array/list to store reachable XOR sums.
    
    % Initialize DP table (M rows, N columns)
    % Each entry will be a list of reachable XOR values.
    
    % Initialize dp_table: list of lists, where each inner list is the set of XOR sums for that cell.
    DP = lists:nth(0, lists:map(fun(Row) -> lists:map(fun(Elem) -> [] end, Row) end, Grid)),

    % Set the starting cell (0, 0)
    StartValue = lists:nth(0, lists:nth(Grid, 0)),
    DP_00 = lists:update(0, 0, [StartValue]),
    
    % Update the first row
    DP_Row0 = lists:lists:foldl(fun(DP_acc, j) ->
        if j == 0 -> DP_acc;
        true ->
            PrevXORs = lists:nth(0, DP_acc), % XOR sums to reach (0, j-1)
            CurrentVal = lists:nth(0, lists:nth(Grid, j)),
            NewXORs = lists:map(fun(X) -> X xor CurrentVal end, PrevXORs),
            lists:update(0, j, NewXORs, DP_acc)
        end
    end, DP, lists:seq(1, N-1)).

    % Update the first column
    DP_Col0 = lists:lists:foldl(fun(DP_acc, i) ->
        if i == 0 -> DP_acc;
        true ->
            PrevXORs = lists:nth(i-1, DP_acc), % XOR sums to reach (i-1, 0)
            CurrentVal = lists:nth(i, lists:nth(Grid, 0)),
            NewXORs = lists:map(fun(X) -> X xor CurrentVal end, PrevXORs),
            lists:update(i, 0, NewXORs, DP_acc)
        end
    end, DP_Row0, lists:seq(1, M-1)).

    % Fill the rest of the DP table
    DP_Final = lists:lists:foldl(fun(DP_acc, i) ->
        if i == 0 -> DP_acc;
        true ->
            Row_i = lists:lists:foldl(fun(Row_acc, j) ->
                if j == 0 -> Row_acc;
                true ->
                    % Get XOR sums from above (i-1, j) and from left (i, j-1)
                    XORs_Up = lists:nth(i-1, DP_acc),
                    XORs_Left = lists:nth(i, DP_acc),
                    
                    Val_ij = lists:nth(i, lists:nth(Grid, j)),
                    
                    % Combine and update
                    NewXORs_Up = lists:map(fun(X) -> X xor Val_ij end, lists:nth(j, XORs_Up)),
                    NewXORs_Left = lists:map(fun(X) -> X xor Val_ij end, lists:nth(j-1, XORs_Left)),
                    
                    % Union of sets
                    CombinedXORs = lists:union(NewXORs_Up, NewXORs_Left),
                    
                    lists:update(i, j, CombinedXORs, Row_acc)
                end
            end, lists:nth(i), lists:seq(1, N-1)),
            lists:update(i, 0, lists:nth(i, DP_Col0), DP_acc)
        end
    end, DP_Col0, lists:seq(1, M-1)).

    % The result is the minimum value in the set of XOR sums at (M-1, N-1)
    ResultXORs = lists:nth(M-1, lists:nth(DP_Final, N-1)),
    
    lists:min(ResultXORs).

%% solve(Grid) -> MinXor
solve(Grid) ->
    M = length(Grid),
    N = if M > 0 then length(lists:at(Grid, 0)) else 0 end,
    
    case {M, N} of
        {0, _} -> 0;
        {_ , 0} -> 0;
        {_ , _} -> 
            % Path length is fixed: (M - 1) + (N - 1) + 1 = M + N - 1 steps.
            % The number of steps is M + N - 2.
            % Since M*N <= 1000, M+N is at most 1001.
            % The maximum path length is small enough for DP/BFS/DFS if state space was small.
            % The state must capture the current XOR sum.
            
            % Max XOR value is 1023 (10 bits).
            % Total states in DP: (M * N) * 2^10. 1000 * 1024 approx 1 million states. This is feasible.
            
            % dp[r][c] will store a set (or map) of all possible XOR sums to reach (r, c).
            % Since we only need the minimum, we can try to maintain the set of reachable XOR values.
            
            % Initialize DP table: a 2D array where each element is a list/set of possible XOR values.
            
            % The maximum possible XOR value is < 1024. We can use a boolean array/map for reachability for a specific XOR value.
            
            % Let's use a map for the DP state: dp[r][c] = {XOR_value_1, XOR_value_2, ...}
            
            % Initialize DP table with empty lists for XOR values
            DP_init = lists:replicate(M, lists:replicate(N, [])),
            
            % Initial state (0, 0)
            Initial_Xor = lists:at(Grid, 0, 0),
            
            DP0 = lists:set_at(DP_init, {0, 0}, [Initial_Xor]),
            
            % Iterate through the grid in topological order (row by row, then column by column)
            DP_final = lists:foldl(fun(Current_DP, {r, c}) ->
                if r =:= 0 and c =:= 0 -> 
                    Current_DP % Already initialized
                else
                    % Calculate reachable XORs for (r, c)
                    
                    % Predecessors are (r-1, c) [from up] and (r, c-1) [from left]
                    
                    % Collect all possible XORs from predecessors
                    Pred_Xors = [],
                    
                    % From Up (r-1, c)
                    if r > 0 ->
                        Up_Xors = lists:at(Current_DP, {r-1, c}),
                        lists:fold(fun(X, Acc) -> [lists:nth(0, Acc) || X] end, []) ->
                            Pred_Xors = lists:append(Pred_Xors, lists:nth(0, Up_Xors)),
                            Pred_Xors
                        end
                    ; true -> Pred_Xors = []
                    end,
                    
                    % From Left (r, c-1)
                    if c > 0 ->
                        Left_Xors = lists:at(Current_DP, {r, c-1}),
                        lists:fold(fun(X, Acc) -> [lists:nth(0, Acc) || X] end, []) ->

                    lists:set_at(Current_DP, {r, c}, New_Xors)

            Final_Xors = lists:at(DP_final, {M-1, N-1}),

                % Update DP table
                lists:set_at(Current_DP, {r, c}, New_Xors)
            end
        end, DP0, lists:seq(M, 0, N, 0)),

    lists:reverse(Acc);
lists:seq_recursive(M, R, N, C, Acc) ->
    Acc =:= [] -> 
        lists:seq_recursive(M, R, N, C+1, [(R, C) | Acc]);
    lists:seq_recursive(M, R, N, C, Acc) ->
        lists:seq_recursive(M, R+1, N, 0, [(R, C) | Acc]).

lists:at(List, {R, C}) ->
    lists:nth(R, List) =:= Row;
    lists:nth(C, Row).

lists:set_at(List, {R, C}, NewValue) ->
    lists:set_at_recursive(List, R, C, NewValue).

lists:set_at_recursive(List, R, C, NewValue) ->
    lists:nth(R, List) =:= Row;
    NewRow = lists:set_at_recursive(lists:nth(R, List), 0, C, NewValue),
    lists:set_at_recursive(List, R, C, NewRow).

lists:lists(Count, Element) ->
    lists:lists_recursive(Count, Element, []).
    
lists:lists_recursive(0, _ , Acc) -> lists:reverse(Acc);
lists:lists_recursive(Count, Element, Acc) ->
    lists:lists_recursive(Count - 1, Element, [Element | Acc]).

lists:head(List) -> lists:case(List) of [H|_] -> H; [] -> error(bad_list) end.
lists:tail(List) -> lists:case(List) of [_|T] -> T; [] -> [] end.

    N = length(Grid !! 0),
    % The problem constraint m * n <= 1000 suggests that a DP approach
    % that stores all possible XOR sums might be feasible, as the number of states
    % (M*N) is small, but the number of paths can be large.
    % Since grid[i][j] <= 1023, the XOR sum is at most 1023 (10 bits).
    % We can use DP where dp[i][j] is the set of all possible XOR sums
    % to reach cell (i, j).

    % Initialize DP table: dp[i][j] = list of XOR sums
    % We use a list of lists structure to represent the 2D array.
    
    % Base case: dp[0][0] = [grid[0][0]]
    InitialRow = [[] | list_to_list(M, N, fun(I, J) -> [] end)],
    dp = [[ [] || _/0 ]] ++ InitialRow,

    % Initialize dp[0][0]
    dp = [[ [Grid !! 0 !! 0] | _ ]] ++ InitialRow,

    % Fill the rest of the DP table
    DpTable = fill_dp(Grid, M, N, dp),

    % The result is the minimum value in DpTable[M-1][N-1]
    FinalXORs = DpTable !! (M - 1) !! (N - 1),
    lists:min(FinalXORs).

%% Helper function to initialize the DP structure based on M and N
list_to_list(M, N, Pred) ->
    lists:foldl(fun(Acc, I) ->
        lists:foldl(fun(RowAcc, J) ->
            Pred(I, J),
            RowAcc
        end, [], [1..N])
    end, [], [1..M]).

%% Recursive function to fill the DP table
fill_dp(Grid, M, N, DP) ->
    % Iterate over rows i from 0 to M-1
    lists:foldl(fun(CurrentDP, I) ->
        % Iterate over columns j from 0 to N-1
        lists:foldl(fun(RowDP, J) ->
            % Skip (0, 0) as it's the base case
            if I =:= 0 and J =:= 0 ->
                RowDP
            else
                % Calculate XOR sums for DP[I][J]
                CurrentCellVal = Grid !! I !! J,
                
                PreviousXORs = case {I, J} of
                    {0, _} -> % Top row (can only come from left)
                        DP !! I !! (J - 1)
                    {_, 0} -> % Left column (can only come from top)
                        DP !! (I - 1) !! J
                    {_, _} -> % General case (from top or left)
                        % Union of XORs from top and left cells
                        list_union(DP !! (I - 1) !! J, DP !! I !! (J - 1))
                end,
                
                NewXORs = lists:map(fun(XOR_Sum) -> XOR_Sum `xor` CurrentCellVal end, PreviousXORs),
                
                % Update DP table: RowDP is DP[I], update element J
                NewRowDP = lists:insert(RowDP, J, NewXORs),
                
                % Update CurrentDP: DP[I] is RowDP, update element J
                lists:insert(CurrentDP, I, NewRowDP)
            end,
            RowDP
        end, CurrentDP, [1..N])
    end, DP, [1..M]).

%% Simple list union function (assuming no duplicate values in the same list for efficiency, though standard union is safer)
list_union(ListA, ListB) ->
    lists:foldl(fun(Acc, X) -> 
        if lists:member(X, Acc) -> Acc; true -> [X|Acc] 
    end, ListA, ListB).

    lists:split(List, Index, {Prefix, Suffix}),
    [Prefix | Element] ++ Suffix.

xor(A, B) ->

%% First half: Paths from (0, 0) to (r, c) where r + c = K.
%% Second half: Paths from (r, c) to (m-1, n-1) where r + c = K. (This requires traversing backwards or careful DP).

%% --- First Half DP ---
%% dp1[r][c] will store a set of all possible XOR sums from (0, 0) to (r, c), where r + c = K.
%% Since r + c = K, we only need one dimension for the DP state if we iterate on K.
%% However, storing sets of XOR values can still be too large if there are many distinct values.

%% Since the maximum XOR value is small (1023), we can use a boolean array or a set of integers for the XOR sums.

%% Let's use a map/set to store the achievable XOR sums at each intermediate point (r, c) where r+c = K.

%% Step 1: Forward traversal (0, 0) -> (r, c) where r+c = K.
%% reachable1[r][c] = Set of XOR sums to reach (r, c).

%% Step 2: Backward traversal (m-1, n-1) -> (r, c) where r+c = K.
%% reachable2[r][c] = Set of XOR sums from (r, c) to (m-1, n-1).
%% The XOR sum from (r, c) to (m-1, n-1) is: grid[r][c] XOR (XOR sum from next step to end).
%% When moving from (r, c) to (r', c'), the path XOR is grid[r][c] XOR ... XOR grid[m-1][n-1].
%% If we define XOR_end(r, c) as the XOR sum *excluding* grid[r][c] for the path starting at (r, c) and ending at (m-1, n-1).
%% Then, the total XOR for a path through (r, c) is XOR_start(r, c) XOR XOR_end(r, c).

%% Let's redefine:
%% XOR_prefix(r, c): XOR sum of path from (0, 0) up to and including grid[r][c].
%% XOR_suffix(r, c): XOR sum of path from grid[r][c] up to and including grid[m-1][n-1].

%% If we meet at (r, c), the total XOR is (XOR_prefix(r, c) XOR XOR_suffix(r, c) XOR grid[r][c]).
%% Because grid[r][c] is included in both prefix and suffix definitions, it gets XORed twice, effectively cancelling out.
%% Total XOR = XOR_prefix(r, c) XOR XOR_suffix(r, c) XOR grid[r][c].
%% Wait, if XOR_prefix includes grid[r][c], and XOR_suffix includes grid[r][c], then:
%% Total = (P_before XOR grid[r][c]) XOR (grid[r][c] XOR S_after)
%% Total = P_before XOR S_after. This is simpler.

%% Let's redefine again:
%% P(r, c): XOR sum of path from (0, 0) up to (r, c) inclusive.
%% S(r, c): XOR sum of path from (r, c) up to (m-1, n-1) inclusive.

%% If we meet at (r, c), the path is P(r, c) combined with the path from (r, c)'s successor to the end.
%% The total XOR is P(r, c) XOR (S(r, c) XOR grid[r][c]).
%% Total XOR = P(r, c) XOR S(r, c) XOR grid[r][c]. (Since grid[r][c] appears in both P and S).

%% Target split point K = floor((m + n - 2) / 2).
%% Intermediate points (r, c) must satisfy r + c = K.

%% --- Implementation details in Erlang ---

%% Since Erlang doesn't have built-in hash sets/maps optimized for this, we will use lists of XOR sums and rely on set operations (though performance might be an issue if the number of paths is huge). Given m*n <= 1000, the number of paths might still exceed feasible limits for the MM approach if the middle line is very wide.

%% Let's re-examine constraints: m*n <= 1000.
%% If m=32, n=32, m*n=1024 (too big). If m=31, n=32, m*n=992.
%% L = 31 + 32 - 1 = 62. K = 30. The middle line has 32 points. The number of paths to any point is C(30, r) which is large.

%% If we stick to the bitwise approach (Greedy determination of minimum XOR):
%% We test bits b = 9 down to 0. We try to keep the b-th bit of the result as 0.
%% Let T_current be the target XOR sum prefix found so far (initially 0).
%% At bit b, we check if there exists a path such that (Path XOR) & ~(2^b - 1) == T_current.
%% I.e., is there a path such that its XOR sum matches T_current in all bits > b, AND its b-th bit is 0?

%% Check function `can_achieve(target_xor)`:
%% Can we reach (m-1, n-1) such that the path XOR equals `target_xor`? (This is DP, too slow if target_xor is the state).

%% We only need to check if *any* path exists whose XOR sum starts with the prefix derived from T_current.

%% Let required_prefix = T_current | (0 << b). (We aim for 0 at bit b)

%% This means we need a DP state that tracks the achievable XOR values *modulo* the current target prefix.
%% Since we are looking for minimum, we only care about whether the resulting XOR is $\le$ the current minimum candidate.

%% Let $T_{candidate} = T_{best} \& \sim(2^b - 1)$. (The prefix of $T_{best}$ down to bit $b$).
%% We check if there exists a path $X$ such that $X \& (\sim(2^b - 1)) == T_{candidate}$.

%% DP State: `dp[r][c]` = Set of achievable XOR values at $(r, c)$.
%% Since the set size can still be up to $2^{10}=1024$, and $m \times n \le 1000$, the DP size is $1000 \times 1024 \approx 10^6$ states. This is feasible if the transitions are fast.

%% State space size: $M \times N \times 2^{10}$. $1000 \times 1024$.
%% Transition: From $(r-1, c)$ and $(r, c-1)$ to $(r, c)$. Merge two sets of size $\le 1024$. O(Set Size).

%% Let $S(r, c)$ be the set of achievable XOR sums at $(r, c)$.
%% $S(0, 0) = \{grid[0][0]\}$.
%% For $(r, c) \ne (0, 0)$:
%% $S(r, c) = \{x \oplus grid[r][c] \mid x \in S(r-1, c) \cup S(r, c-1)\}$.

%% We can represent the set $S(r, c)$ as a boolean array of size 1024, where `bools[x] = true` if $x$ is achievable.

solve(Grid) ->
    M = length(Grid),
    N = length(Grid%%).
    
    % Initialize DP table: dp[r][c] is a list of booleans (size 1024) representing achievable XOR sums.
    % We use a structure to represent this 2D array of bitmasks/booleans.
    
    % In Erlang, a list of lists is easiest for DP structure.
    % dp[r] is a list of size N. dp[r][c] is a list of 1024 booleans.
    
    MaxXor = 1023,
    
    % Initialize DP table: M lists, each containing N lists of 1024 false booleans.
    AllFalse = list_to_binary_list(MaxXor, false), % Dummy helper if needed, but we use lists of booleans.
    InitialRow = list(1024, false),
    DP = list(M, list(N, InitialRow)).
    
    % Set initial state (0, 0)
    G00 = erlang:nth1(1, erlang:nth1(1, Grid)),
    Set00 = list(1024, false),
    list_set(Set00, G00, true),
    
    % Update DP[0][0]
    DP_start = list_set(DP, 0, list_set(list_at(DP, 0, 0), G00, Set00)).

    % Iterate through cells in topological order (r+c increasing)
    DP_final = loop_dp(DP_start, M, N, Grid, MaxXor).
    
    % The result is the minimum XOR sum in DP_final[M-1][N-1]
    FinalSet = list_at(DP_final, M-1, N-1),
    
    MinXor = find_min_xor(FinalSet, MaxXor).

%% --- Helper functions ---

% Helper to safely get an element from a list, assuming 0-indexing logic for internal use
list_at(List, Index, Element) ->
    element(Index, List).

% Helper to safely set an element in a list
list_set(List, Index, Value) ->
    lists:replace(List, Index, Value).

% Recursive DP computation
loop_dp(DP, M, N, Grid, MaxXor) when M > 0 and N > 0 ->
    % Iterate over r from 0 to M-1, c from 0 to N-1
    list:foldl(fun(Acc_DP, r) ->
        list:foldl(fun(Inner_DP, c) ->
            if r =:= 0 and c =:= 0 ->
                % Already initialized
                Inner_DP
            else
                % Calculate S(r, c)
                Grc = erlang:nth1(r+1, erlang:nth1(c+1, Grid)),
                
                Set_r_minus_1_c = if r > 0 -> list_at(Inner_DP, r-1, c) ; undefined,
                Set_r_c_minus_1 = if c > 0 -> list_at(Inner_DP, r, c-1) ; undefined,
                
                NewSet = calculate_next_set(Set_r_minus_1_c, Set_r_c_minus_1, Grc, MaxXor),
                
                % Update DP[r][c]
                Updated_Inner_DP = list_set(Inner_DP, c, NewSet),
                list:set(Acc_DP, r, Updated_Inner_DP)
            end
        end, DP, 0)
    end, DP, 0).

% Calculates S(r, c) = {x ^ Grc | x in S(r-1, c) U S(r, c-1)}
calculate_next_set(Set1, Set2, Grc, MaxXor) ->
    % Start with all false
    NewSet = list(MaxXor+1, false),
    
    % XOR from Set1
    list:foldl(fun(Acc, X) when element(X, Set1) == true ->
        XorVal = X ^ Grc,
        list_set(Acc, XorVal, true)
    end, NewSet, 0)
    % XOR from Set2 (merging into the accumulating set)
    |> fun(Acc1) ->
        list:foldl(fun(Acc, X) when element(X, Set2) == true ->
            XorVal = X ^ Grc,
            list_set(Acc, XorVal, true)
        end, Acc1, 0)
    end.

% Finds the smallest index i for which Set[i] is true
find_min_xor(Set, MaxXor) ->
    list:foldl(fun(Min, Index) ->
        if element(Index, Set) == true and Min == undefined ->
            Index
        end
    end, undefined, 0)
    |> fun(Min) when is_undefined(Min) ->
        MaxXor % Should not happen if the target is reachable
    end.
